Wolverine

[NO_JERV_001]

Author
Affiliation

Anders Kolstad

The Norwegian Institute for Nature Research (NINA)

Published

May 16, 2026

Recommended citation: Kolstad, A. 2026. Wolvering [Jerv] (ID(s): NO_JERV_001) v. 1.0.0. ecRxiv: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/tree/main/indicators/NO_JERV_001

Metadata
Log
  • May 19 2026 - Started work

1. Summary

  1. Background and rationale: Wolverine is a top predator in Norwegian mountains. It is mainly a scavenger, feeding on carcasses ranging from small rodents to reindeer. They require large territories in order to find enough food. Historic over hunting is one reason for the low abundance we see today, but current management through culling is also a major determinant.

  2. Methods: Wolverine numbers are reported each year by Statens Naturoppsyn (SNO) for each of the eight carnivore management regions in Norway. These estimates are associated with relativey small uncertainties. We get the data from the Norwegian Nature Index database (Nater and Eriksen 2025) where also the X100 reference values are stored. These represent the hypothetical number of wolverine in Norwegian alpine ecosystems given minimal anthropogenic influence, and are based lagely on expert opinion.

  3. Interpretation: An indicator value of 1 means that wolverine are as numerous, or more, than what we expect under the reference condition. Values decreasing toward zero indicates that the average population size is decreasing for each carnivore area.

  4. Results and development needs: The indicator value for Norway as a whole in 2024 is 0.06 [95% CI = 0.06-0.07]. This is extremely low and reflects the both the historical overexploitation and current management. The indicator value is highest in eastern Norway, and lowest in the south and western Norway, where it is more or less abscent. Development needs include a revision of the X100 values based on updated knowledge, and a more structured approach to expert elicitation (or other methods).

1.2 Native language summary - Norwegian

  1. Bakgrunn og begrunnelse: Jerv er en topp-predator i norske fjellområder. Den er hovedsakelig åtseleter og lever av kadavre i størrelsen fra smågnagere til rein. Jerv trenger store leveområder for å finne nok mat. Historisk overbeskatning er en viktig årsak til den lave bestanden vi ser i dag, men dagens forvaltning gjennom lisensfelling er også en sentral faktor.

  2. Metoder: Jervebestanden rapporteres hvert år av Statens naturoppsyn (SNO) for hver av de åtte rovviltregionene, og disse estimatene er knyttet til relativt små usikkerheter. Dataene som brukes i denne indikatoren hentes fra Naturindeks-databasen (Nater and Eriksen 2025), hvor også referanseverdiene er lagret. Referanseverdiene representerer det hypotetiske antallet jerv i norske alpine økosystemer under minimal menneskelig påvirkning, og er i stor grad basert på ekspertvurderinger.

  3. Tolkning: En indikatorverdi på 1 betyr at jervebestanden er like stor eller større enn det vi forventer under referansetilstanden. Verdier som nærmer seg null indikerer at den gjennomsnittlige bestandsstørrelsen på rovviltregionsnivå er avtagende og går mot null.

  4. Resultater og utviklingsbehov: Indikatorverdien for Norge som helhet i 2024 er 0,06 [95 % KI = 0,06–0,07]. Dette er svært lavt og reflekterer både historisk jakt og dagens forvaltning. Indikatorverdien er høyest i Øst-Norge og lavest i Sør- og Vest-Norge, hvor jerv er mer eller mindre fraværende. Utviklingsbehov inkluderer en revisjon av X100-verdiene basert på oppdatert kunnskap, samt en mer strukturert tilnærming til ekspertvurderinger (eller andre metoder).

2. About the underlying data

The data comes from the national carnivore monitoring program (www.rovdata.no). Variable estimates are based on Milleret et al. (2024) for 2024, and Bischof et al. (2019) for 2019 and 2014. Estimated for earlier years are partlybased on expert opinion (see the Norwegian Natur index websites)

2.1 Spatial and temporal resolution and extent

Indicator values are available for the different assessment years in Naturindeksen: 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019 and 2024.

The indicator is calculated for eight carnivore regions in Norway. These may overlap with one or more of the geographic regions that we use here. Here we report the values for five geographic regions, and for Norway as a whole.

2.2 Sampling design

The data is collected by ‘Statens Naturoppsyn’ (SNO) by different means. including DNA sampling from feces, hair and urine. The sampling is not systematic, but more or less complete.

2.3 Original units

The original units of the raw data is the number of individuals. The data are assigned to spatial units, so this could be turned into measures of density.

2.4 Instructions for citing, using and accessing data

The underlying data are available via rovdata.no. Indicator values from the Norwegian Nature Index can be found in (Nater et al. 2025). Indicator values, variable values and reference values can be downloaded from the Nature Index database using the API, as I have done below. The data is licenced under CC-BY 4.0.

3. Indicator properties

3.1 Ecosystem Condition Typology Class (ECT)

B1 - Compositional state characteristics

3.2 Ecosystem condition characteristic

The ecosystem characteristic represented in this indicator is an ecosystem where species abundances and composition is similar to that in the reference condition.

3.3 Collinearities with other indicators

None that are very strong.

3.4 Impact factors

Wolvering still suffers from very strong hunting from year 1700 up until it was protected in 1973. The population today is also heavily regulated by culling. Wolverine is also affected by other human activities such as land use change or disturbance.

3.5 Spatial aggregation

A model is used to estimate an aggregated wolvering count at the scale of carnivore regions. Here the variable is normalised by scaling and truncating against X100 and X0. The indicator values are downscaled to the level of municipalities before they are aggregated to the five geographic regions, and then to Norway, with a sample weight proportional to the mountain area in each municipality or region.

4. Reference condition and levels

4.1 Reference condition

The reference condition is a moutain ecosystem with minimal human impact and a climate similar to the period 1961-1990 (Nybø and Evju (red) 2017).

4.2 Reference levels

X100 is defined based on the predicted number of wolverine individuals based on a statistical model (Bischof et al. 2019; Milleret et al. 2024). This value varies between carnivore regions.

X0 is defined as a complete absence of wolverine.

4.2.1 Spatial resolution and validity

The reference values vary between the eight carnivore management regions, and normalisation of variable values is therefore done at this scale as well.

5. Uncertainties

5.1 General uncertainties

One main source of uncertainty is about how the X100 is set using expert opinion, based on assumptions about the amount of suitable habitat and the density of reproducible units(individuals). There is also some uncertainty about the need to define an X60 value. This will, however, have little influence on the indicators values, since they are going to be much closer to the X0value in any case.

5.2 Reported uncertainties

The reported uncertainties reflect the uncertainties around the true parameter means, i.e. they are inferrential uncertainties for the true value of the indicator for the given area and time combination.

6. References

Bischof, R., C. Milleret, P. Dupont, J. Chipperfield, H. Brøseth, and J. Kindberg. 2019. RovQuant: Estimating density, abundance and population dynamics of bears, wolverines and wolves in scandinavia. MINA fagrapport 63: 1–79.
Milleret, C., P. Dupont, H. Brøseth, O. Flagstad, O. Kleven, H. Königsson, G. Spong, J. Kindberg, et al. 2024. Estimates of wolverine density, abundance, and population dynamics in scandinavia, 2015–2024. MINAfagrapport.
Nater, C. R., and L. F. Eriksen. 2025. Naturindeks for Norge 2025. Tilstand og utvikling for biologisk mangfold. {NINA} {Temahefte} 97. Trondheim: Norsk Institutt for Naturforskning.
Nater, C. R., T. Zeiner-Henriksen, and S. Nybø. 2025. NI2025: Indicator Data. doi:10.21345/y790-v762.
Nybø, S., and M. Evju (red). 2017. Fagsystem for fastsetting av god økologisk tilstand - forslag fra et ekspertråd.

7. Import and prepare datasets

7.1 Naturindeks

We download the dataset from the Naturindeks database using the API. The function giver read-only access without requiring a username and password.

Code 
# I use a dev version of NIcalc where the username and passwork for the guest user is hrd coded into the function. This will probably be merged into the real NIcalc (from NINAnor/NIcalc) soon.
# renv::install("ChloeRN/NIcalc@include_ReadOnly_access")
# library(NIcalc)

# Import data set
jervImport <- NIcalc::importDatasetApi(
  eco = NULL,
  indic = c("Jerv"),
  year = c("2010", "2014", "2019", "2024")
)
saveRDS(jervImport, here::here("data/jervImport.rds"))

8. Spatial units

According to the Naturindeks terminology, the 430 municipalities are called BSunits. The Carnivore regions (Rovviltregion) are subsamples of BSunits, and they are referred to as ICunits. The five geographic regions in Norway (east, south, west, central, north) are called parts and NIunits. We can think of the carnivore regions as ecosystem assets, and mainland Norway as the Ecosystem accounting Area.

Code 
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/rovviltregioner.png"))

9. Analyses

Here I use a generic approach to handling data from the Naturindeks where I get both the variable values and the reference values, separately, from the NI database, and perform the normalisation and spatial aggregation ourselves.

The reference values have uncertainties attached to them, which we will ignore here. The variable values have uncertainties as well, in the form of probability function. The uncertainties are from a statistical model, and so we think of them as inferential uncertainties.

Here I use an NIcalc function to just prepare and structure the data a little differently.

Code 
# Assemble
assemble <- NIcalc::assembleNiObject(
  inputData = jervImport,
  predefNIunits = c(allArea = T, parts = T, counties = F),
  indexType = "thematic"
)

Then I get the reference values. These are unique to each carnivore region. I only have an X100 value. X0 is a natural zero.

Code 
x100 <- jervImport$referenceValues$referenceValues |>
  select(ICunitId, ICunitName, x100 = expectedValue)
gt::gt(x100)
ICunitId ICunitName x100
1302 Rovviltregion 8 837
1304 Rovviltregion 7 355
1305 Rovviltregion 6 404
1311 Rovviltregion 1 281
1313 Rovviltregion 2 204
1315 Rovviltregion 3 194
1316 Rovviltregion 5 164
5141 Rovviltregion 4 29

The variable values are stored as a parameterised probability function, and I need to draw n samples from this function. The probability function differs between indicators in the Naturindeks. It could also differ within an indicator. Here I create a vector defining which probability function to use. I use ´tradObs´ where there is a regular probability distribution family, and customObs where customDist!= NA. When distributionFamilyName == NA, customDistribution != NA.

Code 
myYears <- as.character(c(2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
obstype <- NULL

for (i in 1:length(myYears)) {
  obs <- assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName

  obs[!is.na(obs)] <- "tradObs"
  obs[is.na(obs)] <- "customObs"

  obstype[[i]] <- obs
}

Now I am ready to sample the probability distributions. I set n to be 1000.

Code 
dat <- tibble(ICunitId = NULL, value = NULL, year = NULL)

for (i in 1:length(myYears)) {
  #print(i)
  myMat <- NIcalc::sampleObsMat(
    ICunitId = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$ICunitId,
    value = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$expectedValue,
    distrib = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName,
    mu = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter1,
    sig = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter2,
    customDistribution = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$customDistribution,
    obsType = obstype[[i]],
    nsim = 1000
  )
  ICunitIds <- row.names(myMat)
  
  myMat <-  myMat |>
    as_tibble() |>
    add_column(ICunitId = ICunitIds, .before = 1) |>
    pivot_longer(cols = -any_of("ICunitId")) |>
    select(-name) |>
    add_column(year = myYears[i])
  
  dat <- dat |> 
    bind_rows(myMat)
}
head(dat)
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  ICunitId value year 
  <chr>    <dbl> <chr>
1 1302      197. 2010 
2 1302      165. 2010 
3 1302      196. 2010 
4 1302      157. 2010 
5 1302      198. 2010 
6 1302      162. 2010

This is the point in the spatial aggregation where we should normalise against the reference values. So let’s add inn the X100 values.

Code 
dat <- dat |>
  left_join(x100 |> mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId)), by = join_by(ICunitId))

Then we can normalise also the scatter plot with variables against indicator values (Figure 2).

Code 
ind <- ec_normalise(
  variable = dat$value,
  x100 = dat$x100
)

dat <- dat |> add_column(indicator = ind)
Code 
temp <- ec_norm_plot(dat$value, dat$indicator) +
  facet_wrap(.~dat$ICunitName) +
  labs(x = "Variable values (number of individuals)")
ggsave(here::here("img/norm_plot_eng.png"), scale =1.5, plot =temp)
Saving 10.5 x 7.5 in image
Code 
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/norm_plot_eng.png"))

I also export a figure for the Norwegian fact sheet:

Export normalisation function plots 
temp <- ec_norm_plot(dat$value, dat$indicator, lan = "norsk") +
  labs(x = "Antall jerv", y = "Idndikatorverdi") +
  facet_wrap(.~dat$ICunitName)
ggsave(here::here("img/norm_plot_facet.png"), scale =1.5, plot =temp)

temp <- ec_norm_plot(dat$value, dat$indicator, lan = "norsk") +
  labs(x = "Antall jerv", y = "Indikatorverdi")
ggsave(here::here("img/norm_plot.png"), scale =1, plot =temp)

Then we estimate values for each geographic region (N, M, E, S, W). In Naturindeks this is done behind the scenes, and I’m not sure how. I will downscale the indicator values from carnivore regions to BSunits, and then, based on the alpine area of each BSunit, I will upscale from BSunits to NIunits.

Code 
# The link between BSunits and NIunits can be found here:
bs_ni <- as.data.frame(assemble$NIunits) |>
  rownames_to_column(var = "BSunits")
head(bs_ni)
      BSunits wholeArea E S W C N
1      Halden         1 1 0 0 0 0
2        Moss         1 1 0 0 0 0
3   Sarpsborg         1 1 0 0 0 0
4 Fredrikstad         1 1 0 0 0 0
5      Hvaler         1 1 0 0 0 0
6     Aremark         1 1 0 0 0 0
Code 
# And the link between BSunits and ICunits can be found here:
bs_ic <- as.data.frame(assemble$ICunits) |>
  rownames_to_column(var = "BSunits") |>
  rename(ICunits = Jerv)
head(bs_ic)
      BSunits ICunits
1      Halden    5141
2        Moss    5141
3   Sarpsborg    5141
4 Fredrikstad    5141
5      Hvaler    5141
6     Aremark    5141
Code 
# and then I combine them
bs_ni_ic <- bs_ni |>
  left_join(bs_ic, by = join_by(BSunits))
head(bs_ni_ic)
      BSunits wholeArea E S W C N ICunits
1      Halden         1 1 0 0 0 0    5141
2        Moss         1 1 0 0 0 0    5141
3   Sarpsborg         1 1 0 0 0 0    5141
4 Fredrikstad         1 1 0 0 0 0    5141
5      Hvaler         1 1 0 0 0 0    5141
6     Aremark         1 1 0 0 0 0    5141
Code 
# and we also get the alpine area
bs_fjell <- as.data.frame(assemble$BSunits) |>
  select(BSunits = name, Fjell) |>
  mutate(BSunits = as.character(BSunits))
head(bs_fjell)
      BSunits Fjell
1      Halden     0
2        Moss     0
3   Sarpsborg     0
4 Fredrikstad     0
5      Hvaler     0
6     Aremark     0
Code 
# and add that as well
bs_ni_ic2 <- bs_ni_ic |>
  left_join(bs_fjell, by = join_by(BSunits)) |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunits))
head(bs_ni_ic2)
      BSunits wholeArea E S W C N ICunits Fjell ICunitId
1      Halden         1 1 0 0 0 0    5141     0     5141
2        Moss         1 1 0 0 0 0    5141     0     5141
3   Sarpsborg         1 1 0 0 0 0    5141     0     5141
4 Fredrikstad         1 1 0 0 0 0    5141     0     5141
5      Hvaler         1 1 0 0 0 0    5141     0     5141
6     Aremark         1 1 0 0 0 0    5141     0     5141
Code 
tail(bs_ni_ic2)
            BSunits wholeArea E S W C N ICunits     Fjell ICunitId
425          Gamvik         1 0 0 0 0 1    1302 1240.5270     1302
426        Berlevåg         1 0 0 0 0 1    1302 1014.4338     1302
427     Deatnu–Tana         1 0 0 0 0 1    1302 2232.8408     1302
428 Unjárga–Nesseby         1 0 0 0 0 1    1302  599.3075     1302
429       Båtsfjord         1 0 0 0 0 1    1302 1387.0247     1302
430    Sør-Varanger         1 0 0 0 0 1    1302 1242.2696     1302
Code 
# and add the new columns to the main dataset
head(dat)
# A tibble: 6 × 6
  ICunitId value year  ICunitName       x100 indicator
  <chr>    <dbl> <chr> <chr>           <dbl>     <dbl>
1 1302      197. 2010  Rovviltregion 8   837    0.126 
2 1302      165. 2010  Rovviltregion 8   837    0.0836
3 1302      196. 2010  Rovviltregion 8   837    0.126 
4 1302      157. 2010  Rovviltregion 8   837    0.0734
5 1302      198. 2010  Rovviltregion 8   837    0.128 
6 1302      162. 2010  Rovviltregion 8   837    0.0791
Code 
dat_expand <- dat |>
  right_join(
    bs_ni_ic2,
    by = join_by(ICunitId),
    unmatched = "error",
    relationship = "many-to-many"
  )
head(dat_expand)
# A tibble: 6 × 15
  ICunitId value year  ICunitName   x100 indicator BSunits wholeArea     E     S
  <chr>    <dbl> <chr> <chr>       <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>       <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 1302      197. 2010  Rovviltreg…   837     0.126 Harstad         1     0     0
2 1302      197. 2010  Rovviltreg…   837     0.126 Tromsø          1     0     0
3 1302      197. 2010  Rovviltreg…   837     0.126 Kvæfjo…         1     0     0
4 1302      197. 2010  Rovviltreg…   837     0.126 Skånla…         1     0     0
5 1302      197. 2010  Rovviltreg…   837     0.126 Bjarkøy         1     0     0
6 1302      197. 2010  Rovviltreg…   837     0.126 Ibestad         1     0     0
# ℹ 5 more variables: W <dbl>, C <dbl>, N <dbl>, ICunits <int>, Fjell <dbl>
Code 
# and we create a column for the regions
dat_expand2 <- dat_expand |>
  select(-wholeArea) |>
  mutate(across(c(E, S, W, C, N), ~ na_if(., 0))) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(E, S, W, C, N), values_drop_na = T, values_to = "count", names_to = "part") |>
  select(-count)
head(dat_expand2)
# A tibble: 6 × 10
  ICunitId value year  ICunitName    x100 indicator BSunits ICunits  Fjell part 
  <chr>    <dbl> <chr> <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>     <int>  <dbl> <chr>
1 1302      197. 2010  Rovviltregi…   837     0.126 Harstad    1302   76.0 N    
2 1302      197. 2010  Rovviltregi…   837     0.126 Tromsø     1302 1347.  N    
3 1302      197. 2010  Rovviltregi…   837     0.126 Kvæfjo…    1302  198.  N    
4 1302      197. 2010  Rovviltregi…   837     0.126 Skånla…    1302  149.  N    
5 1302      197. 2010  Rovviltregi…   837     0.126 Bjarkøy    1302   18.0 N    
6 1302      197. 2010  Rovviltregi…   837     0.126 Ibestad    1302   67.2 N

Spatial aggregation

Then we can aggregate from municipalities to geographic regions using ecTools. This uses a Monte Carlo propagation of the uncertainties.

This takes a while to run with n = 1000.

Code 
mc_propagated <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = dat_expand2,
  variable = indicator,
  weight = Fjell,
  start_units = BSunits,
  end_units = part,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated, here::here("data/mc_propagated.rds"))

To get the national indicator estimate, we will do another MC propagation from geographic regions to national scale. So we need the summed mountain area per geographic region.

Code 
aux <- dat_expand2 |>
  select(-indicator) |>
  distinct(BSunits, .keep_all = T) |>
  group_by(part) |>
  summarise(alpine_area = sum(Fjell)) 
aux
# A tibble: 5 × 2
  part  alpine_area
  <chr>       <dbl>
1 C          17145.
2 E          17917.
3 N          57452.
4 S           6572.
5 W          19183.
Code 
mc_propagated <- mc_propagated |>
  left_join(aux, by = join_by(part)) |>
  add_column(end_units = "Norway")

mc_propagated2 <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = mc_propagated,
  variable = sampled_mean,
  weight = alpine_area,
  start_units = part,
  end_units = end_units,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated2, here::here("data/mc_propagated2.rds"))

Then we append this to the main dataset

Code 
mc_propagated3 <- mc_propagated |>
  select(year, part, sampled_mean) |>
  bind_rows(mc_propagated2)

10. Results

Then we create some summary statistics (Table 2) and plot them (Figure 3).

Code 
summary_out <- mc_propagated3 |>
  group_by(part, year) |>
  summarise(
    median = round(median(sampled_mean), 2),
    p_025 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.025), 2),
    p_25 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.25), 2),
    p_75 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.75), 2),
    p_975 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.975), 2),
  ) |>
  add_column(
    ECT = "B1",
    Indicator = "Wolverine",
    Ecosystem = "Mountains",
    ID = "NO_JERV_001"
  )
Code 
part_labels <- c(
  N = "North",
  C = "Central",
  W = "West",
  S = "South",
  E = "East",
  Norway = "Norway",
  All = "Norway"
)

region_levels <- c("Norway", "Central", "East", "North", "South", "West")

region_cols <- setNames(
  MetBrewer::met.brewer("Archambault", n = length(region_levels)),
  region_levels
)

plot_data <- summary_out |>
  mutate(
    year = factor(year, levels = myYears),
    series_type = if_else(part == "Norway", "National", "Region"),
    part2 = recode(part, !!!part_labels)
  )
Code 
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = year, y = median, colour = part2, group = part2)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(linewidth = series_type),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  scale_linewidth_manual(
    values = c(National = 2, Region = 0.55),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(ymin = p_025, ymax = p_975, shape = series_type),
    size = 0.8,
    linewidth = 0.45,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = region_cols, name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c(National = 19, Region = 17),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  ) +
  guides(
    colour = guide_legend(
      nrow = 2,
      override.aes = list(size = 0.7, linewidth = 0.45)
    ),
    shape = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 0.8, linewidth = 0.45))
  ) +
  labs(y = "Indicator value", x = "")
Code 
summary_out |>
  unite(col = "CI_95", c(p_025, p_975), sep = " - ") |>
  unite(col = "IQR", c(p_25, p_75), sep = " - ") |>
  select(
    ECT,
    Indicator,
    ID,
    "Accounting area" = part,
    Ecosystem,
    "Closing year" = year,
    "Indicator value" = median,
    "95% CI" = CI_95,
    "50% CI" = IQR
  ) |>
  knitr::kable()

11. Export file

The indicator values are exported and can be found under data/results_NO_PTAR_001_v1.0.0.csv.

Export data 
write.csv(mc_propagated3, here::here("data/results_NO_JERV_001_v1.0.0.csv"), row.names = F)

12. Author contributions

Not relevant, as this is the work of a single author. I would, however, like to acknowledge the work of those that created the indicator on Naturindeks (Nater and Eriksen 2025).

Session Info

This workflow uses renv. Run renv::restore() to recreate the working environment.
